title: Steak frites
subtitle: Bistro ribeye, twice-fried chips, butter that holds.
excerpt: The Parisian café plate: a ribeye seared in a heavy pan, twice-fried chips, and a knob of compound butter melting over the top. Hot pan, room-temperature meat, salt at the last moment. Serves two.
sourceNotes: The bistro plate as documented in French café tradition. Twice-frying technique standard across French and Belgian frites canon. Ribeye doneness and resting times follow conventional steak guidance.

content.text: A hot heavy pan, a piece of steak left at room temperature for half an hour, salt put on at the last second. Chips fried twice: once at 140°C to cook through, once at 180°C to crisp. A knob of garlic-parsley butter sitting on the meat as it rests. This is the café plate as it has been served in Paris for a hundred years.
Two things matter most. The steak goes in a properly hot pan, not a warm one. The chips get a rest between fries so the steam works its way out.

items.name: Ribeye steak
items.prepNote: two thick steaks, ~2.5 cm, at room temperature

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: Maris Piper or King Edward, peeled, cut into 1 cm batons

content.text: You need a heavy pan that holds heat: a cast-iron skillet is ideal. A deep saucepan or fryer for the chips, and an instant-read thermometer so the oil sits where it should.

Mash {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter with {{garlic}} of chopped garlic, {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley, and the lemon zest. Pile onto a square of greaseproof paper and roll into a log about as thick as your thumb. Twist the ends and put it in the freezer for ten minutes.

Rinse {{potato}} of cut potatoes in cold water until the water runs clear. Dry thoroughly on a clean tea towel; wet chips spit in oil. Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil to 140°C in a deep saucepan. Lower the chips in carefully in batches and cook for 6 to 8 minutes until soft but pale. Lift out with a slotted spoon onto a tray and let them rest while you cook the steak.

Pat the steaks dry. Season heavily on both sides with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of salt flakes and {{black-pepper}} of pepper just before they hit the pan. Heat a cast-iron skillet over the highest setting until you can see a faint haze. Add {{vegetable-oil}} of oil; it should shimmer instantly. Lay the steaks in away from you and don't move them. Three minutes one side, then turn and three minutes the other for medium-rare on a 2.5 cm steak. Use the tongs to stand each steak on its fat edge for 30 seconds at the end so the fat browns.
Lift onto a warm plate. Cut a thick slice of the chilled butter and lay it on each steak. Cover loosely with foil and rest for 8 minutes.
Bring the oil back up to 180°C. Drop the chips in for 2 to 3 minutes until properly golden and crisp. Lift onto kitchen paper, season with sea salt flakes the moment they come out of the oil.

items.fix: Bring the steak to room temperature for 30 minutes. Heat the pan until you can see a faint haze before the steak goes in.
items.cause: Pan not hot enough or steak too cold from the fridge.
items.symptom: Steak grey and weeping, no crust

items.fix: Use a thermometer and check the oil reads 180°C. Dry the cut potatoes thoroughly on a tea towel before they go in.
items.cause: Oil not hot enough for the second fry, or chips not dried after rinsing.

items.fix: The 140°C first fry cooks the potato through. Don't go straight to 180°C.
For a 2.5 cm ribeye, 3 minutes per side gives medium-rare. Use an instant-read thermometer if unsure: 52°C in the centre is medium-rare.
items.cause: Too long in the pan, or steak too thin.
items.symptom: Steak overcooked, no pink in the middle

items.fix: Keep the butter log in the freezer until the steak is resting. Slice straight from frozen.
items.cause: Pulled out of the freezer too early.

content.text: Sirloin or rump works in place of ribeye; both are leaner so the pan stays drier. The garlic-parsley butter can take half a teaspoon of Dijon mustard or chopped shallot in place of the lemon zest. For a true bistro à l'os version, use a bone-in côte de boeuf and add five minutes to the cooking time.

Steak frites is the plat du jour of every neighbourhood café from Pigalle to Saint-Germain. Order it at a brasserie like Le Relais de l'Entrecôte and the version arrives with a green salad and a herb-shallot butter that nobody will tell you the recipe for. At home it pairs with a glass of light Burgundy and a sharp dressed salad to cut the richness.
